pillar: Android home-screen widgets — Jetpack Glance (androidx.glance) vs classic RemoteViews
summary: Jetpack Glance is production-ready and has been since 1.0.0 (Sept 2023). Current stable is 1.1.1 (Oct 2024, includes the CVE-2024-7254 protobuf security fix); 1.2.0-rc01 shipped Dec 2025 and 1.3.0-alpha is in flight (July 2026). It is Google's officially recommended, actively maintained way to build app widgets with Compose-style Kotlin, endorsed in the Android docs and Now in Android. It does NOT replace RemoteViews — it compiles down to RemoteViews under the hood, so RemoteViews' hard limits still apply (no gradients, no arbitrary nesting, ListView-backed lazy lists). What Glance fixes for glass-todo specifically is exactly the class of bug you have: unreliable taps. Glance's actionRunCallback<T> + ActionParameters replaces the fragile setPendingIntentTemplate + setOnClickFillInIntent mechanism that is the root cause of checkbox/add taps intermittently doing nothing. Callbacks run in a WorkManager worker with a ~10-minute suspend budget instead of a ~10s BroadcastReceiver, and each list item gets its own type-safe, individually-registered action rather than relying on OEM-flaky fill-in-intent extra merging. Recommendation: migrate the widget layer to Glance 1.1.1 (or 1.2.0-rc01 for the newest APIs), keep SupabaseTodosRest, QuickAddActivity and the RLS/auth logic unchanged, and add optimistic local state via updateAppWidgetState so the checkbox flips instantly before the network round-trip completes.
recommended_approach: Replace TodoWidgetProvider (AppWidgetProvider) + TodoWidgetService (RemoteViewsService/Factory) + the two widget_*.xml layouts with: (1) a TodoGlanceWidget: GlanceAppWidget that fetches todos in the suspend provideGlance() and renders a LazyColumn of Glance composables; (2) a TodoGlanceReceiver: GlanceAppWidgetReceiver registered in the manifest; (3) a ToggleTodoAction: ActionCallback wired to each row's CheckBox via onCheckedChange = actionRunCallback<ToggleTodoAction>(actionParametersOf(idKey to t.id)); and (4) the header add button as Image/Button with onClick = actionStartActivity<QuickAddActivity>(). Keep SupabaseTodosRest.kt, SupabaseClient, QuickAddActivity, TodoSyncWorker (just swap notifyAppWidgetViewDataChanged for TodoGlanceWidget().updateAll(context)), and the appwidget-provider XML (point initialLayout to Glance's default loading layout). Add optimistic UI: on toggle, write the new done-state into the widget's Preferences state via updateAppWidgetState + update() first, THEN do the network PATCH, so the UI never feels dead. Use SizeMode.Responsive for clean resizing. Gradle: add androidx.glance:glance-appwidget:1.1.1 and androidx.glance:glance-material3:1.1.1, ensure the Compose compiler plugin is enabled and minSdk >= 23.
versions: androidx.glance:glance-appwidget 1.1.1 — latest STABLE, released 2024-10-16, includes CVE-2024-7254 protobuf 4.28.2 security fix (use this for production)
androidx.glance:glance-material3 1.1.1 — Material 3 + dynamic color interop, same track as stable
androidx.glance:glance-appwidget 1.2.0-rc01 — released 2025-12-03, release candidate with newest APIs (FontFamily support, refinements); safe to trial, not yet final
1.0.0 — first stable, 2023-09-06 (Glance has been production-ready since this date)
1.3.0-alpha01 2026-05-19 / 1.3.0-alpha02 2026-07-01 — experimental (Wear widgets, health data); do NOT use in production
Requires: Kotlin Compose compiler plugin enabled, minSdk 23 (Android 6.0), Jetpack Compose runtime
best_practices: Use actionRunCallback<T>() + actionParametersOf() for ALL interaction — it is the single biggest reliability win over RemoteViews fill-in intents; each action is type-safe and individually registered, so no OEM-flaky extra-merging.
For a CheckBox, read the new checked value inside the callback via parameters[ToggleableStateKey] and pass the row id with your own ActionParameters.Key<String>; do not recompute state from stale UI.
Fetch app data in the suspend provideGlance() (or a repository Flow) — it runs off the main thread with a generous budget, unlike the ~20s RemoteViewsFactory.onDataSetChanged window.

Do OPTIMISTIC updates: updateAppWidgetState { it[key]=newValue } then update(context, glanceId) to re-render instantly, and only afterwards perform the network write in the same suspend callback. This removes the 'tap does nothing' perception entirely.
Trigger refresh with TodoGlanceWidget().updateAll(context) from WorkManager / after inserts, instead of AppWidgetManager.notifyAppWidgetViewDataChanged.
Use SizeMode.Responsive(setOf(DpSize...)) for smooth resize; read LocalSize.current to adapt layout. Prefer Responsive over Exact (Exact fully recreates the tree on every resize).
Use GlanceTheme { } (from glance-material3) for Material You dynamic color on Android 12+; use ColorProvider(day=..., night=...) for light/dark where you need explicit colors.
Pass resource IDs directly (ImageProvider(R.drawable...), background(R.color...)) rather than runtime Color where possible — smaller RemoteViews payload and dynamic-resource support.
Keep the appwidget-provider XML; set initialLayout to @layout/glance_default_loading_layout (shipped by the library) so there is a clean first frame while provideGlance runs.
Register a GlanceAppWidgetReceiver in the manifest (it IS an AppWidgetProvider subclass) — you can keep android:updatePeriodMillis and WorkManager exactly as today.
Provide unique item ids to items(items, itemId = { it.id.hashCode().toLong() }) so list diffing/scroll position is stable across updates.

code_patterns.what: Gradle deps (module build.gradle.kts) — add Glance, keep Compose enabled
TodoGlanceWidget — fetch in suspend provideGlance(), render LazyColumn with per-row CheckBox action (THE reliable tap fix)
ToggleTodoAction — optimistic flip then network PATCH, reads new state from ToggleableStateKey
TodoGlanceReceiver + manifest registration (replaces AppWidgetProvider)
code_patterns.snippet:
class TodoGlanceReceiver : GlanceAppWidgetReceiver() {
  override val glanceAppWidget: GlanceAppWidget = TodoGlanceWidget()
  override fun onEnabled(context: Context) { /* enqueue TodoSyncWorker as today */ }
}

<!-- AndroidManifest.xml -->
<receiver android:name=".widget.TodoGlanceReceiver" android:exported="false">
  <intent-filter><action
     android:name="android.appwidget.action.APPWIDGET_UPDATE"/></intent-filter>
  <meta-data android:name="android.appwidget.provider"
     android:resource="@xml/todo_widget_info"/>
</receiver>

code_patterns.what: Optional: true optimistic paint via Preferences state (instant checkbox before network)
appwidget-provider XML — reuse yours, point initialLayout to Glance's loading layout
code_patterns.snippet:
<appwidget-provider ...
  android:initialLayout="@layout/glance_default_loading_layout"
  android:targetCellWidth="3" android:targetCellHeight="2"
  android:resizeMode="horizontal|vertical"
  android:widgetCategory="home_screen" />

our_current_mistakes: ROOT CAUSE of unreliable taps: TodoWidgetProvider.updateWidget uses setPendingIntentTemplate(MUTABLE broadcast) + TodoWidgetService.getViewAt setOnClickFillInIntent. Fill-in-intent extra-merging into a shared template is notoriously flaky across OEMs/launchers — when the merge silently fails, the tap fires the template with no id/done extras and onReceive returns early at `getStringExtra(EXTRA_ID) ?: return`, so the checkbox appears dead. Glance's per-item actionRunCallback removes this entire mechanism.

No optimistic UI: a tap does NOT change anything visible until onReceive completes a network PATCH AND notifyAppWidgetViewDataChanged forces onDataSetChanged to re-fetch the WHOLE list over the network. On a slow/failed connection the row never visibly flips → reads as 'didn't respond'. There is no local state to paint immediately.

Every single toggle triggers a full list refetch: onReceive -> notifyAll -> onDataSetChanged -> SupabaseTodosRest.fetch() (runBlocking network). Rapid taps queue behind blocking network calls and get dropped; during the in-flight window new taps are effectively ignored.

The toggle runs inside a BroadcastReceiver (onReceive) with only ~10s even with goAsync(); a slow Supabase round-trip can be killed mid-flight, leaving the DB updated-or-not ambiguously and the widget stale. Glance callbacks run in a WorkManager worker with a ~10-minute suspend budget.

Whole-row tap toggles done (setOnClickFillInIntent on item_root) — there is no distinct checkbox hit target, and any mis-merge of the fill-in intent kills the only interaction the row has. A real CheckBox composable in Glance is a first-class, individually-actioned target.

The '+' add button is actually implemented correctly (getActivity + FLAG_IMMUTABLE, avoiding the Android 12 broadcast->activity trampoline ban) — but it shares the same RemoteViews update cycle, so if updateWidget isn't re-run the intent can go stale. In Glance, actionStartActivity is re-registered on every recomposition, so it can't rot.

hasStableIds()/getItemId use id.hashCode() which is fine, but collection RemoteViews caching plus no-op getLoadingView (returns null) can leave stale rows painted after a toggle — compounding the 'nothing happened' feel.
pitfalls: Glance compiles to RemoteViews, so RemoteViews limits REMAIN: no gradients (your widget_glass_bg gradient look must become a flat/rounded ColorProvider background — the 'glass' aesthetic is not reproducible in a widget), no arbitrary nesting, no ConstraintLayout, limited Box alignment.
LazyColumn translates to a ListView with all collection restrictions; NO nested lists/scroll, and very large lists still incur RemoteViews action registration cost per item.

Glance composables are NOT interoperable with regular Jetpack Compose — you cannot reuse your app's TaskRow/GlassSurface/AuroraBackground composables inside the widget; it is a separate composable world.
Typography is constrained: FontFamily support was only added in newer versions (1.2.0-rc01) and custom fonts/letterSpacing/advanced text styling you use in-app won't fully carry over.
Previews and the emulator lie: many issues (sizing, click behavior, OEM launcher quirks) only appear on real devices — test toggles on a physical phone, ideally Samsung/Xiaomi launchers.
updateAll re-runs provideGlance which re-fetches from network on every refresh; without caching/optimistic state you can reintroduce lag — pair it with Preferences state or a repository cache.
Do not start Activities from a lambda .clickable {} on Android 12+ — use actionStartActivity<...>() (you already need this for the add button).
Pin the version: use 1.1.1 stable (has the CVE-2024-7254 fix) — 1.1.0 is vulnerable; avoid 1.3.0-alpha in production.
Glance requires minSdk 23; glass-todo must not target lower. Also requires the Compose compiler plugin configured in the widget module.

State restore: widgets are stateless/passive and can be recreated any time — never hold widget state in in-memory app scope; persist via updateAppWidgetState (Preferences DataStore) or your data layer.

pillar: Offline-first reactive MVVM: Room as local single-source-of-truth → Flow → StateFlow → immutable UiState → Compose, with optimistic writes reconciled/rolled back against Supabase in the background.
summary: The v1 code (TodoScreen.kt, MainActivity.kt, TodoRepository.kt) uses the classic anti-pattern the official Android guidance warns against: a one-shot imperative pull (repo.list() inside LaunchedEffect(Unit)) into composable-local mutableStateOf, then hand-mutating that List on every toggle/add. There is no ViewModel, no local database, no Flow, no offline support, no loading/error/empty distinction, and every optimistic edit silently loses on server failure because runCatching{} swallows the error and nothing rolls back. The 2026 recommended architecture inverts this: Room is the single source of truth, the DAO exposes Flow<List<TodoEntity>>, the Repository coordinates Room + Supabase, the ViewModel turns that Flow into an immutable TodoUiState via stateIn(WhileSubscribed(5000)), and Compose renders it with collectAsStateWithLifecycle. Writes hit Room first (instant UI reaction via Flow), then push to Supabase in the background; on permanent failure the Room row is reverted (rollback), otherwise it stays PENDING for WorkManager retry. This structurally eliminates the jank (stale screen after widget/other-device edits, blank screen on airplane mode, lost toggles, double network round-trip on add) because the UI is a pure function of one observable store rather than a snapshot mutated by hand.
recommended_approach: Layer the app as UI → ViewModel → Repository → (Room local SSOT + Supabase remote). 1) Room DAO exposes fun observeAll(): Flow<List<TodoEntity>> — the ONLY read path the UI ever sees. 2) TodoViewModel maps that Flow into an immutable TodoUiState and exposes it as StateFlow via .stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue = TodoUiState(isLoading = true)); combine() folds in isSyncing and a transient error signal. 3) Compose collects with collectAsStateWithLifecycle() and renders explicit loading/empty/content branches plus a snackbar for errorMessage. 4) Every mutation is optimistic: write to Room (client-generated UUID for offline creates, syncStatus=PENDING, tombstone flag for deletes) → Flow instantly re-emits → UI updates with zero network wait → then push to Supabase; onSuccess mark SYNCED, onFailure either revert the Room row (rollback for permanent errors) or leave it PENDING so WorkManager retries when connectivity returns. 5) refresh() pulls Supabase and upserts into Room; errors surface as a non-blocking banner, never a blank screen, because Room still has the last-known data. Business logic (the #project parsing) moves from the composable into the ViewModel where it is unit-testable. Keep Room 2.8.3 (stable, Room 2 is in maintenance but fully supported) rather than jumping to Room 3.0 for an Android-only app.
best_practices: Room DAO returns Flow, not List: fun observeAll(): Flow<List<TodoEntity>>. Room re-emits automatically on any table change, so widget QuickAdd, TodoSyncWorker, and remote pulls all propagate to the screen with zero extra wiring.

UiState is a single immutable data class with val fields (todos, isLoading, isSyncing, errorMessage) and derived flags (val isEmpty get() = !isLoading && todos.isEmpty()). One state object = one consistent frame, per the official 'UI state must be immutable' rule.

Produce StateFlow with stateIn(scope=viewModelScope, started=SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5_000), initialValue=...). WhileSubscribed(5000) stops upstream Room/Flow collection ~5s after the UI stops observing (backgrounded) and restarts on return, surviving config-change without re-querying.
Collect in Compose with collectAsStateWithLifecycle() (from lifecycle-runtime-compose, already in your toml) so collection pauses in STOPPED state — not plain collectAsState().
State survives rotation and process death because it lives in the ViewModel (viewModelScope), not in remember{} inside the composable. Text-in-progress can go in SavedStateHandle.
Optimistic write order: mutate Room first (UI reacts via Flow instantly), then reconcile with the server. Never block the UI on the network round-trip.
Offline creates need a client-generated UUID (not a Postgres-assigned id) so the row exists locally before the server ever sees it; reconcile by keeping the same id server-side.

Model sync state explicitly on the row (syncStatus = SYNCED | PENDING, plus a deleted tombstone) so a background WorkManager job can find and push everything still PENDING when connectivity returns — soft-delete by default (matches your rule 6).
Structured concurrency: all writes run in viewModelScope (cancelled with the ViewModel) and hop to Dispatchers.IO inside the repository, not on rememberCoroutineScope() tied to composition.

Surface errors as a transient one-shot (Channel<UiEvent> or a nullable errorMessage cleared after showing) → Snackbar, distinct from the loading and empty states. Never let a failed fetch look like an empty list.
Move business logic (the #proyecto regex parsing, title cleanup) out of the composable into the ViewModel/a pure function so it is unit-testable without Compose.

Testability: the ViewModel takes the Repository as a constructor param; the Repository takes DAO + remote interfaces. Use an in-memory Room DB (Room.inMemoryDatabaseBuilder) and a fake remote in tests; drive uiState with Turbine and runTest.

code_patterns.what: Immutable UI state + view model (StateFlow via stateIn/WhileSubscribed, combine of Room Flow + sync + error). Replaces composable-local mutableStateOf.
Room entity + DAO exposing Flow (the reactive single source of truth). This is what makes repo.list()-in-LaunchedEffect obsolete.
Offline-first repository: optimistic add/toggle/delete with reconcile-or-rollback. Room is written first (UI reacts via Flow), Supabase pushed after.
Compose screen: explicit loading/empty/content states, lifecycle-aware collection, no hand-mutation. Replaces the whole TodoScreen state block.
Gradle: KSP plugin + Room/lifecycle-viewmodel-compose/datastore additions to libs.versions.toml.

our_current_mistakes: TodoScreen.kt:54 — LaunchedEffect(Unit){ tasks = repo.list() } is a one-shot imperative pull. It runs once; when the widget's QuickAddActivity, TodoSyncWorker, or another device changes data, the screen NEVER updates. This is exactly the non-reactive pattern the target architecture removes.

TodoScreen.kt:51 — state lives in remember{ mutableStateOf<List<Todo>>() } inside the composable. It does not survive configuration change or process death: rotate the phone and the whole list re-fetches from the network (flash of empty content) and the half-typed 'input' text is lost.

TodoScreen.kt:54 — runCatching{ repo.list() }.getOrDefault(emptyList()) collapses 'network failed' and 'genuinely empty' into the same blank screen. There is no isLoading, no error, no empty distinction — the three states the user must be able to tell apart.

TodoScreen.kt:68-72 — toggle does an optimistic hand-mutation (tasks = tasks.map{...}) then scope.launch{ runCatching{ repo.setDone() } }. The failure is swallowed: if the server rejects it, the UI stays toggled, the DB never changed, and the next reload silently reverts. A lost update the user never sees. There is NO rollback.

TodoScreen.kt:126-129 — add() calls repo.add() then tasks = repo.list(): two sequential network round-trips per add, and the new row doesn't appear until BOTH succeed. Offline, nothing appears and the error is eaten. No optimistic insert.
TodoScreen.kt:120-123 — the #proyecto regex parsing and title cleanup (business logic) live inside the IconButton onClick in the composable, so they can't be unit-tested without Compose.
TodoScreen.kt:50/71/125 — writes are launched on rememberCoroutineScope() (tied to composition), not a ViewModel scope. Wrong layer and wrong lifecycle for data mutations.

TodoRepository.kt — it is not a single source of truth; it's a thin Supabase RPC wrapper. list() returns a List<Todo> snapshot, not Flow<List<Todo>>, which is what forces the pull-once-and-hand-mutate pattern upstream. No Room, no cache, no offline: airplane mode = empty screen.

TodoRepository.kt:16 — uid() uses currentUserOrNull()!!; if the session expires mid-use this throws and (combined with the swallowing runCatching in the UI) shows an empty list instead of a re-auth prompt.
TodoRepository.kt — setDone/add/delete return Unit with no server echo, so the caller has nothing to reconcile optimistic UI against; there is no conflict resolution or pending-op queue.

MainActivity.kt:41 — repo is created by lazy in the Activity (manual DI, no ViewModel). signedIn and all screen state are held in composable remember{}, so process death / rotation rebuilds everything. There is no ViewModelStoreOwner retaining state across the config change.

There is no local persistence at all in the app-read path (Room is absent from libs.versions.toml), so the app is online-only despite already having a background TodoSyncWorker — the sync layer has no local store to sync into.
pitfalls: Don't put the whole optimistic-then-rollback dance in the UI. With Room as SSOT the 'rollback' is a single dao.upsert(prev) inside the repository; the UI just observes. Keeping rollback logic in the composable recreates the v1 bug.
stateIn's initialValue must represent 'loading' (isLoading=true), not an empty list — otherwise the first frame looks like a genuinely-empty todo list and the empty-state flashes before Room emits.
WhileSubscribed(0) or Eagerly defeats the purpose: 0 cancels the Room flow immediately on every recomposition gap; Eagerly keeps querying while backgrounded. Use WhileSubscribed(5_000).

Offline creates MUST use a client-generated UUID as the primary key. If you rely on Postgres to assign the id (your TodoInsert has no id), the row can't exist locally before the network call, so you can't be optimistic or offline.

Deletes must be soft (tombstone + PENDING) then hard-deleted only after the server confirms — a hard local delete before the push means a failed push can't roll back and the row is gone forever. Matches your rule 6 (soft-delete by default).

Rapid repeated toggles fire overlapping network writes; debounce or coalesce, and always reconcile against the server echo, or the last in-flight response can clobber a newer local value. Storing updatedAt lets you drop stale responses.

Don't expose the Room TodoEntity to Compose. Map to a UI model (TodoUi); leaking sync/tombstone columns into the UI couples the screen to persistence and breaks recomposition skipping when internal fields change.

Room 3.0 (2026) is Kotlin-first/KSP-only/KMP and drops the SupportSQLite API — do NOT adopt it reflexively for an Android-only app; Room 2.8.3 is stable and fully supported (Room 2 is in maintenance = still patched). Only move to Room 3 if you go multiplatform.
KSP version must be pinned to the Kotlin version (2.2.20 → 2.2.20-2.0.x). A mismatch fails the build. Room 2.8 uses KSP, not kapt.
Use collectAsStateWithLifecycle(), not collectAsState(); the latter keeps collecting while the app is in STOPPED state, wasting the WhileSubscribed savings and risking work off-screen.

composeBom = "2026.06.01" in your toml has a 'verify it resolves' comment — confirm the exact BOM is published before wiring viewmodel-compose against it, or pin lifecycle-viewmodel-compose explicitly (it's versioned independently of the Compose BOM anyway).
Don't reintroduce a manual refresh-after-write (the tasks = repo.list() line). With a Room Flow, any write re-emits automatically; an explicit re-read is redundant and races the Flow.
versions: Room 2.8.3 (stable, released 2025-10-22; Room 2 in maintenance mode — still patched). Use room-runtime + room-ktx + room-compiler via KSP.

Room 3.0 (2026, InfoQ): Kotlin-first, KSP-only (no kapt/Java), coroutine-first (suspend/Flow DAOs), drops SupportSQLite for androidx.sqlite driver, adds KMP incl. JS/Wasm. Migration bridge: room3-sqlite-wrapper. Adopt only if going multiplatform.

androidx.lifecycle 2.9.1 (stable; 2.10.0 also in stable channel) — provides lifecycle-viewmodel-compose (viewModel() in Compose) and lifecycle-runtime-compose (collectAsStateWithLifecycle). Your toml has 2.9.0; bump.
androidx.datastore:datastore-preferences 1.1.7 (stable) — for session flags / simple prefs instead of ad-hoc state.
KSP 2.2.20-2.0.x — must match your Kotlin 2.2.20.
androidx.work:work-runtime-ktx 2.9.1 — already in your toml; use it to drain PENDING rows to Supabase on reconnect.
Your existing stack that stays: Compose BOM 2026.06.01 (verify it resolves — flagged in your own toml), activity-compose 1.11.0, Supabase BOM 3.6.0, Ktor 3.4.0, kotlinx-serialization 1.8.0.
sources: https://developer.android.com/topic/architecture — Guide to app architecture (data layer = SSOT, UI→VM→Repository)
https://developer.android.com/topic/architecture/ui-layer/state-production — UI State production (stateIn, WhileSubscribed(5000), immutable state)
https://developer.android.com/topic/architecture/ui-layer — UI layer (observable immutable UiState via StateFlow)

pillar: supabase-kt Realtime + Auth + offline-first (Room) for native Kotlin/Android, with a widget that shares one client
summary: The v1 app polls Supabase every 15 min via WorkManager and reads the widget's data through a raw-OkHttp REST helper (SupabaseTodosRest) that pulls the JWT from the shared supabase-kt Auth plugin. The "widget shows empty" bug is NOT primarily a token-load race that awaitInitialization fixes — awaitInitialization only waits for the plugin's first load. The real root cause: by default supabase-kt's Auth plugin registers Android lifecycle callbacks that CLEAR the in-memory session and disable auto-refresh when the process goes to background. A widget refreshed by WorkManager/binder while the app is backgrounded therefore sees currentAccessTokenOrNull()==null, falls back to ANON, and RLS returns nothing. The maintainers' documented fix is enableLifecycleCallbacks=false in the Auth config (then you own refresh, but the session stays in memory and loads from encrypted storage at init). Separately, the library already ships the primitive this app should be built on: selectAsFlow / postgresChangeFlow (realtime-kt), which merges the initial PostgREST select with a live postgres_changes stream into a Flow<List<Todo>>. That Flow is the correct feed into Room, making Room the single source of truth that both the Compose UI and the widget observe — replacing both the 15-min poll and the raw-OkHttp widget path. realtime-kt is currently NOT a dependency, so none of this exists yet.
recommended_approach:
Move to an offline-first, realtime-fed architecture with Room as the single source of truth.

1) CLIENT (data/SupabaseClient.kt): install(Realtime) alongside Auth and Postgrest (add the realtime-kt dependency — it's missing). Set enableLifecycleCallbacks=false in install(Auth) to stop the background session-clearing that causes the widget's anon fallback; keep autoLoadFromStorage=true (default) so the persisted session is restored at process start. Because lifecycle auto-refresh is now off, drive refresh yourself: Postgrest 3.3+ has a session failsafe that force-refreshes an expired token on a request, and realtime re-auths via the Auth token, but still schedule refreshCurrentSession() (in the WorkManager job and on app resume) to keep the widget's token fresh.

2) AUTH GATING: replace repo.isSignedIn() (synchronous currentUserOrNull() that can race storage load) with collecting auth.sessionStatus. Render Loading on Initializing, todo screen on Authenticated, sign-in on NotAuthenticated, and — per the maintainers' offline-auth guidance — treat RefreshFailure/network errors as "still authenticated, show cached Room data with an offline badge" rather than signing out. Use awaitInitialization() only as a one-shot guard in non-composable entry points (widget/worker).

3) REALTIME→ROOM: build the live list with client.from("todos").selectAsFlow(Todo::id) which returns Flow<List<Todo>> (initial select + postgres_changes merged, keyed by PK). Collect it in a repository/ViewModel scope and upsert into a Room @Dao (replace-all reconcile, or per-row from postgresChangeFlow<PostgresAction> for insert/update/delete granularity). UI and widget both observe Room's Flow, so they update live off one WebSocket instead of polling. Server side: add the table to the supabase_realtime publication and set REPLICA IDENTITY FULL so UPDATE/DELETE payloads carry the row and RLS applies to the stream.

4) WIDGET: stop using raw OkHttp + runBlocking for reads. RemoteViewsFactory.onDataSetChanged should read from Room (fast synchronous DAO query on the binder thread), because Room is kept current by the app's realtime collector + the WorkManager refresh. Keep writes (toggle/insert) on the shared supabase-kt Postgrest client (suspend, inside goAsync/CoroutineWorker) instead of hand-built JSON. If a cold-start network read is needed, route it through client.from("todos").select().decodeList() so it inherits the same token resolution and retries — don't keep a second divergent REST layer.

5) CRUD ROBUSTNESS: keep suspend repo functions; rely on Postgrest 3.6 automatic retries for transient errors, and distinguish auth-fail (RestException 401/403 → refresh/re-auth, do NOT delete data) from data-fail (validation/constraint → surface to user). Do optimistic Room updates, then reconcile from the realtime Flow.
best_practices: Install Realtime, Auth, and Postgrest in ONE createSupabaseClient and share it process-wide (the app already does this via App.onCreate + the SupabaseClient object); realtime uses the Auth plugin's token automatically and re-auths the socket on refresh.

Set Auth { enableLifecycleCallbacks = false } for any app whose data is read in the background (widgets, workers). This is the maintainers' documented fix for 'background data retrieval not working' — the default clears the session from memory on ON_STOP.

With lifecycle callbacks off, own token refresh: call auth.refreshCurrentSession() on resume and inside the widget's WorkManager job; Postgrest's session failsafe also force-refreshes an expired token on demand.

Drive UI from auth.sessionStatus (Initializing / Authenticated / NotAuthenticated / RefreshFailure) collected with collectAsStateWithLifecycle — never from a one-shot currentUserOrNull() read that can precede storage load.
Use awaitInitialization() ONLY at non-UI entry points (widget factory, worker) to guarantee the session finished loading before reading the token.

Model the live list as client.from("todos").selectAsFlow(Todo::id) → Flow<List<Todo>>; it merges the initial select with postgres_changes for offline-capable initial data plus live updates from one API.
Make Room the single source of truth: realtime Flow writes Room, UI and widget both observe Room — instant cold-start, offline reads, one reconciliation point.
Scope realtime collection to a long-lived scope (repository/ViewModel); selectAsFlow subscribes on start and removes the channel on completion, so short-lived scopes thrash the socket.
Server: add the table to the supabase_realtime publication and set REPLICA IDENTITY FULL so UPDATE/DELETE realtime payloads include the row and RLS applies.
Treat NetworkError/RefreshFailure as 'authenticated with stale data' (show cached Room + offline indicator), not sign-out — matches the maintainers' offline-auth discussion.
Keep @Serializable data classes matching the table; postgresChangeFlow<PostgresAction.Insert>().decodeRecord<Todo>() decodes rows directly.
Use the supabase-kt client for ALL Supabase I/O; avoid a parallel hand-rolled OkHttp/JSON layer that duplicates token logic and drifts from RLS/retry behavior.

code_patterns.what: Client init: add Realtime, fix the widget background race with enableLifecycleCallbacks=false
Offline-first live list: Supabase realtime Flow -> Room (single source of truth)
Granular change handling if you don't use selectAsFlow
Auth gating from sessionStatus (replaces racy repo.isSignedIn())
Widget factory reads from Room (no raw OkHttp, no runBlocking network)
CRUD with auth-fail vs data-fail distinction + manual refresh (lifecycle callbacks off)

our_current_mistakes: realtime-kt is not a dependency and Realtime is never installed — the app has no live-update capability at all; the 15-min WorkManager poll is the only sync path. This is the headline gap.

The widget's empty-list bug is misdiagnosed in the code comments. currentAccessTokenBlocking() already calls awaitInitialization(), so 'session hasn't loaded yet' is NOT the live cause. The real cause is the default enableLifecycleCallbacks=true: backgrounded, Auth clears the in-memory session, so a WorkManager/binder refresh reads null token -> ANON -> RLS returns nothing. Fix is enableLifecycleCallbacks=false, not more awaiting.

SupabaseTodosRest.kt is a second, hand-rolled data layer (raw OkHttp + manual JSON string-building in insert()/setDone()) that duplicates and diverges from the client's token resolution, retries, and serialization. Delete it in favor of the shared client (reads from Room, writes via Postgrest).

runBlocking(Dispatchers.IO) with awaitInitialization() on the widget binder thread (TodoWidgetService.onDataSetChanged, SupabaseClient.currentAccessTokenBlocking) blocks a limited binder thread on network + possible token refresh; with Room as source of truth the read becomes a fast local query and the blocking network call disappears.

No local persistence at all — TodoScreen holds tasks in Compose state and re-fetches via repo.list() on every add; no Room means no offline reads, no instant cold start, and app+widget keep separate in-memory copies that disagree.

MainActivity uses repo.isSignedIn() (synchronous currentUserOrNull()) to choose the screen; it can run before the persisted session finishes loading, briefly showing sign-in to a logged-in user. Gate on auth.sessionStatus with an Initializing state instead.

TodoRepository.list()/setDone()/add() have no realtime feedback loop: a change from another device or the widget is invisible to the open app until the next manual list(). selectAsFlow makes the open list self-updating.
Manual JSON in the widget REST insert() (jsonStr escaping by hand) is fragile; kotlinx.serialization via the client handles this safely.
No REPLICA IDENTITY FULL / publication note anywhere — even after adding Realtime, UPDATE/DELETE payloads would be thin and Room reconciliation of deletes would break without it.
pitfalls: enableLifecycleCallbacks=false disables the library's automatic background refresh AND lifecycle-driven reload — you must call auth.refreshCurrentSession() yourself (on resume and in the widget worker) or tokens silently expire and the widget 401s.
postgres_changes requires the table in the supabase_realtime publication; without it the channel subscribes fine but never emits.

Without REPLICA IDENTITY FULL, DELETE (and filtered UPDATE) realtime payloads only carry the primary key / partial row, so decodeRecord<Todo>() on deletes fails and RLS may not filter the stream correctly.

Realtime respects RLS only when the socket is authenticated with the user JWT; if the session isn't loaded when you subscribe (same background-clearing issue), the channel authorizes as anon and emits nothing. awaitInitialization() before subscribe() guards this.

selectAsFlow subscribes on collection start and removes the channel on completion — collecting it in a short-lived scope (e.g. a recomposing LaunchedEffect) thrashes the WebSocket. Scope it to repository/ViewModel.

Don't keep BOTH a 15-min WorkManager poll and realtime for the foreground app — they double-write Room and cause flicker. Keep the worker only as a low-frequency safety net / token-refresh trigger for the widget while the app is dead.
Postgrest 3.6 auto-retries transient errors but NOT auth failures — catch RestException 401/403 separately and refresh/re-auth rather than retrying blindly or wiping local data.

Widgets can't hold a long-lived WebSocket; realtime belongs to the app process. The widget relies on Room being updated by the app's realtime collector plus a periodic worker for the app-killed case — don't open a realtime channel from the RemoteViewsFactory.

New Supabase API keys (sb_publishable_ / sb_secret_) are replacing anon/service keys by end of 2026; keep the publishable/anon key client-side, never the secret key (the existing code comment is correct).
versions: supabase-kt BOM: 3.6.0 stable (currently pinned in libs.versions.toml, good); 3.7.0-beta-1 available (binary broadcasts, httpSend, Realtime VSN 2.0.0)
Modules resolved by BOM: realtime-kt (ADD THIS — missing), postgrest-kt, auth-kt — version-aligned by the BOM, no per-module version

Fixes already in 3.6.0: Postgrest automatic retries for transient errors (3.6), Realtime consecutive-connects fix (3.6), channels no longer tear down during reconnects (3.4.1), Android background auto-refresh fix (3.2.5), session failsafe force-refresh of expired tokens (3.3)
ktor 3.4.0 (client-okhttp engine) — compatible
kotlinx-serialization-json 1.8.0, Kotlin 2.2.20 — compatible
Add Room ~2.7.x (KSP) for the offline-first store: androidx.room:room-runtime + room-ktx (Flow DAOs) + KSP compiler
Supabase key migration: publishable (sb_publishable_) / secret (sb_secret_) replacing anon/service by end of 2026; client stays on publishable/anon

pillar: Android home-screen widget ↔ app state synchronization (Jetpack Glance + Room/DataStore + WorkManager)
summary: The reliable pattern is a single source of truth (Room, optionally fronted by a DataStore cache for widget-only UI state) that BOTH the app and the widget read — the widget never owns its own fetch. Data flows one way: something writes to Room → the widget re-reads Room. Immediate freshness comes from the writer explicitly poking the widget (Glance `updateAll`/`update(glanceId)`, or classic `notifyAppWidgetViewDataChanged`) right after it commits data; background/periodic freshness comes from a WorkManager `CoroutineWorker` that fetches on a proper Dispatcher (never the binder thread, never `runBlocking` in a `RemoteViewsFactory`), writes to Room, then pokes the widget. In Glance you observe the Room `Flow` with `collectAsState` INSIDE `provideContent`, so a recomposition always re-pulls current DB state. The current design (widget doing its own OkHttp fetch + app doing separate SDK calls with no shared cache + `runBlocking` in the factory) is fragile because there are two divergent copies of the truth, the widget blocks a binder thread on the network (ANR/ dropped update), and neither side can invalidate the other.
recommended_approach:
Adopt a unidirectional, single-source-of-truth architecture:

1) TRUTH = Room. One `@Entity` + a DAO that exposes `Flow<T>` for reads and suspend writers for updates. Every fetch result lands here first. Never let the widget hold a second, independently-fetched copy.

2) WIDGET-ONLY UI STATE = Glance `GlanceStateDefinition` backed by (Proto/Preferences) DataStore — use ONLY for per-widget things like a toggle, selected tab, last-refresh timestamp. Do NOT duplicate domain data here; keep domain data in Room.

3) READ PATH (Glance): inside `provideContent { }` call `repository.stream.collectAsState(initial)`. Because Glance recomposes on that Flow emission, any Room write automatically redraws the widget — no manual push needed for the in-process case, and manual pushes make cross-process/backgrounded cases instant.

4) WRITE PATH: exactly one writer per event. App foreground SDK call, FCM push handler, user action, and the periodic Worker all funnel into `repository.refresh()` which (a) does network OFF the main/binder thread, (b) upserts into Room, (c) calls `MyWidget().updateAll(context)` (or `update(context, glanceId)` for one instance, or `updateIf` by state).

5) BACKGROUND/PERIODIC = WorkManager `CoroutineWorker`. `PeriodicWorkRequest` (≥15 min) with `Constraints` (NetworkType.CONNECTED, optionally battery-not-low), exponential backoff, and `enqueueUniquePeriodicWork(name, KEEP or UPDATE, request)` so you never stack duplicate chains. The worker calls the same `repository.refresh()`. Prefer WorkManager over `updatePeriodMillis` because many OEMs ignore that XML attribute.

6) THREADING: `update/updateAll/updateIf` and `provideContent` collectors are suspend/coroutine — run them off the main thread (CoroutineWorker already gives you that). NEVER do network with `runBlocking` inside `RemoteViewsFactory.onDataSetChanged/getViewAt` (binder thread → ANR-class stalls and dropped frames). If you keep a classic collection widget, `onDataSetChanged` may block, but it must block only on a fast local Room read, not on OkHttp.

Migration for your current setup: delete the widget's OkHttp client; route the app's SDK calls through the same repository that writes Room; replace the widget's fetch-on-render with `collectAsState` over the Room Flow; add one `CoroutineWorker` for periodic refresh; call `updateAll` from the repository after every write.
best_practices: Single source of truth = Room. The widget and the app read the SAME DAO Flow; the widget owns NO fetching logic of its own.
Use DataStore + GlanceStateDefinition ONLY for widget-local UI state (toggles, selected id, last-refresh ts), never for domain data that the app also needs — that stays in Room.
Observe the Room Flow with collectAsState INSIDE provideContent/Content(), not outside it — collectors created outside the composition miss emissions and the widget goes stale.

After any write to Room, have the writer call MyWidget().updateAll(context) (all instances), update(context, glanceId) (one), or updateIf<State>{} (conditional). This makes backgrounded/cross-process widgets refresh instantly instead of waiting for the next system tick.
Do all network in a CoroutineWorker (or app-side coroutine), write to Room, THEN update the widget. The worker guarantees you are off the main/binder thread.

Periodic refresh via WorkManager PeriodicWorkRequest with min 15-min interval, NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint, and exponential backoff — not android:updatePeriodMillis, which OEMs frequently ignore.

Use enqueueUniquePeriodicWork with a stable name + ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.KEEP (or UPDATE in WorkManager 2.8+ to change params without losing the schedule) to avoid stacking duplicate periodic chains on every app launch.
For instant push freshness use FCM data messages / app foreground events to trigger the same repository.refresh() — treat them as just another writer, not a separate code path.

For classic (non-Glance) collection widgets, call AppWidgetManager.notifyAppWidgetViewDataChanged(ids, R.id.list) to invalidate the collection; onDataSetChanged then re-reads Room. Keep that read local and fast.
Guard against widget-deleted races: catch IllegalStateException around update/runComposition, and clean up GlanceState in onDelete/deleted().
Compare old vs new state before pushing an update (updateIf, or a diff in updateAppWidgetState) to skip redundant recompositions and save battery.
Register the CoroutineWorker/HiltWorkerFactory correctly (or use a default factory) and initialize WorkManager once; a misconfigured factory silently fails the worker.

code_patterns.what: Room as the single source of truth: Flow read + suspend upsert
Repository: the ONLY place network happens; one refresh() that all writers call, off the main thread, writes Room, then pokes the widget
Glance widget observes the Room Flow INSIDE provideContent — auto-recomposes on every DB write, no fetching of its own
WorkManager periodic refresh: CoroutineWorker (off binder thread) + constraints + backoff + unique work
Classic (non-Glance) collection widget done RIGHT: invalidate via notifyAppWidgetViewDataChanged; factory reads LOCAL Room only, never network
GlanceStateDefinition (DataStore) for WIDGET-LOCAL UI state only

our_current_mistakes: The widget runs its OWN OkHttp fetch. This creates a second, independent copy of the truth that can (and will) diverge from the app's copy, and there is no shared cache to reconcile them.
The app makes SEPARATE SDK calls with no shared cache/repository. App and widget can show different data for the same moment, and neither can invalidate the other.

runBlocking on a network call inside RemoteViewsFactory (onDataSetChanged/getViewAt) blocks a binder thread on I/O — ANR-class stalls, dropped/rolled-back widget updates, and jank. onDataSetChanged may block, but only on a fast LOCAL read, never on the network.
No single source of truth: without Room (or equivalent) fronting both readers, there is no place a write can land that BOTH surfaces observe, so 'update once, appear everywhere' is impossible.

Likely relying on implicit/system update ticks (or updatePeriodMillis) instead of an explicit updateAll after each write, so the widget lags the app and is stale on OEMs that throttle background updates.
pitfalls: Collecting the data Flow OUTSIDE provideContent/Content() — the collector isn't tied to the composition, misses emissions, and the widget silently goes stale.
Doing network in onDataSetChanged/getViewAt (binder thread) — even with runBlocking it risks ANR and dropped updates; move network to a CoroutineWorker and write Room first.
Calling suspend update/updateAll from the main thread or a non-coroutine context — must run in a coroutine/worker off the main thread.
Enqueuing the PeriodicWorkRequest on every app start without enqueueUniquePeriodicWork — you stack duplicate chains that multiply network calls and battery drain.
Expecting sub-15-minute periodic refresh from WorkManager — the floor is 15 min; for real-time use event-driven pushes (FCM/user action/updateAll), not shorter periods.
Trusting android:updatePeriodMillis for freshness — many OEM battery managers ignore it; treat WorkManager + explicit updateAll as the real mechanism.
Widget-deleted race: update()/runComposition() on a just-removed instance throws IllegalStateException — wrap in try/catch and clean GlanceState in onDelete/deleted().

Glance's session lock can hold ~45–50s, so back-to-back updateAll calls for truly real-time UI (media player) can lag; for that niche only, the documented runComposition + AppWidgetManager.updateAppWidget bypass exists — but it's a hack, avoid unless you need millisecond updates.
Pushing an update when nothing changed wastes battery — use updateIf or diff old vs new in updateAppWidgetState before updating.
Misconfigured WorkManager factory (custom/Hilt worker without the right WorkerFactory) makes doWork silently never run — verify the worker actually executes.
versions: Jetpack Glance 1.1.x (stable, 2024–2025) — provideGlance/provideContent API, GlanceAppWidgetManager, updateAll/update/updateIf, runComposition
androidx.work (WorkManager) 2.9–2.10 — PeriodicWorkRequest 15-min floor, ExistingPeriodicWorkPolicy.UPDATE added in 2.8, Constraints, BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL
Room 2.6+ — Flow reads, @Upsert
DataStore (Preferences/Proto) 1.1+ — backs Glance GlanceStateDefinition for widget-local state

pillar: Automated testing of an Android app + home-screen widget in CI (GitHub Actions) without a physical device
summary: You can go from "build blind" to "verified in CI" with a two-tier test pyramid that needs ZERO physical device. Tier 1 (fast, runs on the plain JVM in ~seconds, no emulator): JUnit unit tests for logic, ViewModel tests with Turbine, Compose UI tests via createComposeRule, and — the highest-value-per-minute layer — Roborazzi screenshot tests (Robolectric-based) that render your Compose screens AND your Glance widget on the JVM and fail the build on any visual/render regression. Tier 2 (slow, ~5-10 min, only when you need real system behavior): reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner boots a headless emulator on GitHub's free Ubuntu runners (KVM-accelerated) to run instrumented Espresso/UIAutomator tests, including physically pinning your widget to the launcher and tapping its buttons. Run Tier 1 on every push as a required check; run Tier 2 on PRs/nightly. Roborazzi is what stops the bug-shipping fastest because it catches "the widget renders wrong / the toggle looks off / the layout broke" in a 30-second JVM job, and its recorded PNGs are committed as golden images so any pixel drift is a reviewable diff.
recommended_approach: Build TWO GitHub Actions jobs. JOB A "jvm" (ubuntu-latest, no emulator): runs ./gradlew test verifyRoborazziDebug lint detekt — this covers unit tests, ViewModel+Turbine, Compose createComposeRule tests, Glance unit tests, and Roborazzi screenshot verification of both Compose screens and the Glance widget. Make this a REQUIRED status check; it is fast and deterministic. JOB B "instrumented" (ubuntu-latest + reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner@v2, KVM enabled, AVD snapshot cached): runs ./gradlew connectedCheck for Espresso + a UIAutomator test that pins the widget and taps it. For the widget specifically, use a layered strategy: (1) Roborazzi to screenshot the GlanceAppWidget content on JVM (fastest render-bug catcher), (2) androidx.glance:glance-appwidget-testing runGlanceAppWidgetUnitTest to unit-test the widget's composables and its actionRunCallback click actions (verifies "tapping toggles state" logic with no launcher), (3) one UIAutomator instrumented test that calls AppWidgetManager.requestPinAppWidget, taps the launcher's confirm dialog, then taps the on-widget button to prove the end-to-end add+toggle really works on a real launcher. Adopt in that order — do (1) and (2) first because they are JVM-fast and catch ~80% of widget bugs; add (3) last because launcher UI is the flakiest surface. Record golden images with ./gradlew recordRoborazziDebug locally (or in CI once), commit them, then CI runs verify.
best_practices: Pin the action to a major tag: reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner@v2 (current v2.38.0). Enable KVM explicitly on ubuntu-latest with the udev rule step, or the emulator falls back to software rendering and is far slower.

Cache the AVD snapshot: a first 'create AVD + boot' job with force-avd-creation:false writes a snapshot to actions/cache (key includes api-level+arch+target), and the test job reuses it — cuts emulator cold-start from minutes to seconds.

Use headless emulator flags: -no-window -gpu swiftshader_indirect -noaudio -no-boot-anim -no-snapshot-save -camera-back none, and set disable-animations: true on the test run so Espresso/UIAutomator don't race animations.

Standardize Roborazzi device qualifiers in an @Config(qualifiers=RobolectricDeviceQualifiers.Pixel5) (or explicit w/h/dpi) so golden images are byte-reproducible across machines; set robolectric.pixelCopyRenderMode=hardware and @GraphicsMode(NATIVE) for accurate rendering.
Turn on android.testOptions.unitTests.isIncludeAndroidResources = true — Roborazzi/Robolectric need real resources merged in.
Separate CI into a required fast JVM check (unit + Roborazzi verify + lint/detekt) and a slower emulator check; never block merges only on the flaky emulator job.

Test Glance widget LOGIC with runGlanceAppWidgetUnitTest (glance-appwidget-testing 1.1.1) using assertHasActionRunCallbackClickAction — it verifies taps wire to the right callback without any launcher, so it never flakes.

Commit golden PNGs to git and treat a Roborazzi diff like a code diff in PR review; store build/outputs/roborazzi/ and the *_compare.png artifacts via actions/upload-artifact so failures are inspectable.

Pick a stable emulator image for UIAutomator launcher tests: google_apis (has a real launcher) on x86_64, a fixed api-level (e.g. 34) — the pin-widget confirm dialog text varies by launcher, so lock the image.
Use a matrix over api-level for the emulator job to catch API-specific bugs, but keep the JVM Roborazzi job single-config for speed.

code_patterns.what: GitHub Actions workflow: fast JVM job (unit + Roborazzi + lint/detekt) as required check, plus emulator job with KVM + AVD caching
code_patterns.snippet:
name: android-ci
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  jvm:            # fast, no emulator — make this the REQUIRED check
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
      - uses: actions/setup-java@v5
        with: { distribution: temurin, java-version: '17' }
      - uses: gradle/actions/setup-gradle@v5
      - name: Unit + ViewModel(Turbine) + Compose + Glance unit + Roborazzi verify
        run: ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest verifyRoborazziDebug lintDebug detekt
      - name: Upload screenshot diffs on failure
        if: failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with: { name: roborazzi-diffs, path: '**/build/outputs/roborazzi/**' }

  instrumented:  # slow — Espresso + UIAutomator widget test on a headless emulator
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix: { api-level: [34] }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
      - uses: actions/setup-java@v5
        with: { distribution: temurin, java-version: '17' }
      - uses: gradle/actions/setup-gradle@v5
      - name: Enable KVM
        run: |
          echo 'KERNEL=="kvm", GROUP="kvm", MODE="0666", OPTIONS+="static_node=kvm"' | sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/99-kvm4all.rules
          sudo udevadm control --reload-rules
          sudo udevadm trigger --name-match=kvm
      - name: AVD cache
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        id: avd-cache
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.android/avd/*
            ~/.android/adb*
          key: avd-${{ matrix.api-level }}
      - name: Create AVD + snapshot (only on cache miss)
        if: steps.avd-cache.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'
        uses: reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner@v2
        with:
          api-level: ${{ matrix.api-level }}
          arch: x86_64
          target: google_apis
          force-avd-creation: false
          emulator-options: -no-window -gpu swiftshader_indirect -noaudio -no-boot-anim -camera-back none
          disable-animations: false
          script: echo "AVD snapshot cached."
      - name: Run instrumented (Espresso + UIAutomator widget)
        uses: reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner@v2
        with:
          api-level: ${{ matrix.api-level }}
          arch: x86_64
          target: google_apis
          force-avd-creation: false
          emulator-options: -no-snapshot-save -no-window -gpu swiftshader_indirect -noaudio -no-boot-anim -camera-back none
          disable-animations: true
          script: ./gradlew connectedDebugAndroidTest

code_patterns.what: Gradle setup for Roborazzi (module build.gradle.kts) — v1.67.0
Roborazzi screenshot test of a Compose screen (JVM, no emulator)
Roborazzi screenshot of a Glance widget's Compose content (JVM) — catches widget render bugs fast
Glance widget logic/action unit test — verifies 'tapping toggles/adds' with NO launcher (glance-appwidget-testing 1.1.1)
UIAutomator instrumented test — pin the widget to the launcher, then tap its button (end-to-end, on emulator)
ViewModel unit test with Turbine (1.2.1) — assert emitted UI states
Plain JUnit unit test for logic (version compare) — the kind of bug that never needs a device

our_current_mistakes: Shipping with zero automated tests because you can't run Android locally — every regression reaches production. The fix is CI-side testing; you never need the device on your machine.

Treating 'I can't run the emulator' as 'I can't test', when in fact the JVM tier (unit + Turbine + Compose createComposeRule + Roborazzi + Glance unit tests) runs entirely on GitHub's runners with no emulator at all.
No screenshot/golden tests, so visual and widget-render bugs (wrong layout, broken toggle state, clipped Glance content) are invisible until a user reports them.
Likely no separation of widget UI code from the GlanceAppWidget, which makes the widget untestable — extract the widget's UI into a plain @Composable so Roborazzi and glance unit tests can render it.
If any emulator CI exists, it probably has no KVM step and no AVD caching, making it slow and flaky enough that it gets ignored rather than trusted.
Manual 'looks fine on my screenshot' verification instead of committed golden PNGs that CI diffs on every push.
pitfalls: Emulator flakiness: without KVM enabled the emulator runs in software mode and times out; without disable-animations:true Espresso/UIAutomator race animations and fail intermittently.
AVD cache key must include api-level + arch + target; a mismatched or missing key silently rebuilds the AVD every run (slow) or restores a wrong image (boot failures).

Roborazzi goldens are environment-sensitive: different device qualifiers, DPI, JDK font rendering, or robolectric.pixelCopyRenderMode produce diffs. Lock @Config qualifiers and record goldens in the SAME environment CI verifies in (ideally record once in CI), or you get spurious failures.

Paparazzi is a viable alternative but lags: it renders via Android Studio layoutlib (tied to AGP/AS releases), is incompatible with Robolectric, can't test Activity/Fragment/interactions, and does NOT render Glance @Previews reliably without widthDp — for a widget app Roborazzi is the better default. Latest Paparazzi ~1.3.x.

UIAutomator widget-pin is the flakiest surface: the launcher's pin-confirm dialog text/layout differs across launchers and API levels (Launcher3 vs Pixel launcher), so By.text matches break. Lock the emulator image (target: google_apis, fixed api-level) or bypass the launcher entirely with AppWidgetHost.bindAppWidgetIdIfAllowed.
By.res(pkg, id) needs stable resource IDs on the widget's RemoteViews/views; Compose/Glance nodes need testTag or contentDescription set or UIAutomator/Compose finders won't locate them.
runGlanceAppWidgetUnitTest has a 1-second default timeout per test — pass a longer duration if your composable does async work, or it fails opaquely.
Don't gate merges on the emulator job alone — it will occasionally flake; make the JVM job the required check and treat the emulator job as advisory/nightly plus PR.
arm64 emulator images on GitHub's x86_64 Linux runners are unsupported/slow — use arch: x86_64 with google_apis or aosp_atd (ATD images are lighter/faster for headless CI).
versions: reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner: v2.38.0 (pin as @v2)
Roborazzi: 1.67.0 (gradle plugin + roborazzi/roborazzi-compose/roborazzi-junit-rule)
Robolectric: 4.14.x
androidx.glance:glance-testing + glance-appwidget-testing: 1.1.1
cashapp/turbine: 1.2.1
Paparazzi (alternative): ~1.3.x (lags AGP/layoutlib; weaker Glance support)
Compose test: via compose-bom (ui-test-junit4)
GitHub Actions: actions/checkout@v6, setup-java@v5 (JDK 17), gradle/actions/setup-gradle@v5, actions/cache@v4

pillar: Premium "liquid glass" Compose to-do app + home-screen widget: correct Haze glass, Material 3 Expressive spring motion, tasteful microanimations, toggle haptics, intentional empty/loading/error states, and a RemoteViews/Glance widget pushed as far as the sandbox allows.
summary: v1 is architecturally sound and, crucially, avoids the #1 liquid-glass trap: it does NOT blur every scrolling row. Only two static surfaces (top bar + bottom input) call hazeEffect via GlassSurface.glass(); TaskRow uses a cheap translucent card. That is exactly the documented Haze performance pattern and deserves credit. Haze 1.7.2 (in libs.versions.toml) is the current STABLE release as of mid-2026 (2.0.0-alpha03 is the only newer build, still alpha), so the API in use — hazeSource/hazeEffect/HazeMaterials + blurRadius/tints/noiseFactor set directly in the trailing lambda — is correct and current. Do NOT migrate to 2.0's blurEffect{}-wrapper/haze-blur-module API yet. The gaps that separate this from "premium": (1) zero haptics — Compose 1.8+ ships HapticFeedbackType.ToggleOn/ToggleOff built for exactly the checkbox toggle, and TaskCheck never fires one; (2) motion is hand-typed spring/tween literals rather than the M3 Expressive MotionScheme (spatial vs effects springs, fast/default/slow); (3) no loading/error/empty states in the app — first launch shows a bare aurora and every failure is swallowed by runCatching, which reads as broken, not intentional; (4) the glass is one flat style with no progressive fade, no specular top-edge highlight, no inputScale; (5) AuroraBackground drives all three blobs off ONE shared phase scalar so they move in lockstep and look mechanical; (6) the widget has no Material You dynamic color and no completed/relative-time polish. Nothing is broken — it's a strong v1 needing polish passes on motion, feedback, and state.
recommended_approach: Keep the Haze topology exactly as-is (correct pattern) and layer polish in five passes. PASS 1 Motion: pull MotionScheme into GlassTheme (MaterialTheme exposes it once you're on material3 1.5.0-alpha or the alpha Compose BOM) and replace hand-typed springs with MaterialTheme.motionScheme.fastSpatialSpec()/defaultEffectsSpec(); use spatial springs (bouncy) for position/size/appearance and effects springs (no overshoot) for color/alpha. PASS 2 Haptics: inject LocalHapticFeedback and fire ToggleOn when a task is checked, ToggleOff when unchecked, plus a Confirm on add. PASS 3 Glass depth: give the two bars distinct materials (ultraThin for the top bar, thin/regular for the input), add a 1px top specular highlight border with a vertical white-to-transparent gradient, add HazeInputScale.Auto, and optionally a HazeProgressive.verticalGradient so the blur fades toward the content edge (mask is the cheaper alternative). PASS 4 States: build explicit Loading (shimmer skeleton rows), Empty (illustrative, not just text), and Error (retry) states driven by a sealed UiState instead of a raw List<Todo> + swallowed runCatching. PASS 5 Widget: keep RemoteViews (Glance also cannot blur, so it buys little here) but add Material You via @android:color/system_accent1_* on API 31+ with values-v31 fallbacks, tighten the glass gradient, add relative due time, and ship a real previewImage for API<31. Stay on stable material3 1.4.0 if you want zero alpha risk and hand-roll the spring specs to match Expressive values; move to 1.5.0-alpha only to get the real MotionScheme + expressive components.
versions: Haze 1.7.2 — CURRENT STABLE (Feb 2026); dev.chrisbanes.haze:haze + haze-materials. Keep it. 2.0.0-alpha03 (Jun 2026) is alpha only: it splits blur into a haze-blur module, moves classes to dev.chrisbanes.haze.blur, wraps blur props in blurEffect{}, renames HazeTint→HazeColorEffect, and removes rememberHazeState(blurEnabled). Do not adopt in a shipping app yet.
Compose BOM: 2026.06.00/2026.06.01 is the latest (the project's "2026.06.01" resolves). BOM maps material3 to STABLE 1.4.0.

androidx.compose.material3: STABLE 1.4.0 (baseline M3). Material 3 Expressive (MotionScheme.expressive(), expressive components) lives in 1.5.0-alpha (…-alpha23 by Jun 2026) behind @ExperimentalMaterial3ExpressiveApi. To use the real MotionScheme you must opt into the alpha.
Compose UI 1.8+: adds HapticFeedbackType.ToggleOn, ToggleOff, Confirm, Reject, GestureThresholdActivate, SegmentTick, etc. Already covered by the 2026.06 BOM.
AGP 8.9.1, Kotlin 2.2.20, compileSdk/targetSdk 36, minSdk 26 — Haze's RenderEffect blur needs API 31; on 26–30 Haze degrades to a translucent scrim (fine, but design for it).

Glance (androidx.glance:glance-appwidget ~1.2.x, 2026) if you ever move the widget off RemoteViews: gives GlanceTheme + GlanceTheme.colors dynamic color, cornerRadius (API31+), but still NO blur and NO gradient-brush background (needs a drawable/ImageProvider).
best_practices: Blur few, static surfaces — never scrolling rows. v1 already does this: only the top bar and bottom input call hazeEffect; TaskRow is a plain translucent card. Haze's own benchmarks show per-item hazeSource lists cost ~+45% frame time vs ~+29% for a simple scaffold. This is the single most important liquid-glass perf rule and it's already followed.

Order matters: clip THEN hazeEffect. To get rounded-corner blur, the rounded clip must come before the blur modifier. The bottom bar does this (.clip(RoundedCornerShape(24.dp)).glass(...)) — correct. The TopAppBar is intentionally edge-to-edge so it needs no clip.

Everything the glass samples must be BOTH drawn behind the effect AND marked hazeSource. v1 marks both AuroraBackground and the LazyColumn as hazeSource so the bars frost over the aurora and the scrolling content — correct and intended.
Use HazeInputScale.Auto on glass over scrolling content; 0.66 fixed cuts ~55% of blurred pixels and is visually imperceptible. Benefit is modest (~5–20% of Haze's own cost) but free to add.

Prefer mask over progressive when you only need an edge fade — Haze docs: masking is ~+5% (negligible), progressive costs more (though the newer custom blur shader made it near-free). Use progressive only when you need a true varying blur radius.

Drive ALL motion from MaterialTheme.motionScheme, not literals: spatial springs (with overshoot) for position/size/enter-exit, effects springs (no overshoot) for color/opacity/blur. This is the core M3 Expressive rule — springs replace duration+easing.

Fire the right haptic for the semantic action: HapticFeedbackType.ToggleOn/ToggleOff on the checkbox (not a generic LongPress), Confirm on successful add, Reject on a blocked/invalid action. These landed in Compose 1.8 specifically so you stop hand-rolling Vibrator patterns.
animateItem needs a stable key — v1 correctly passes key = { it.id } to items(). Keep it; without it add/remove/reorder animations are wrong.

Animate the checkbox via graphicsLayer scale, not layout offset — v1's TaskCheck already uses graphicsLayer { scaleX/scaleY } so the check doesn't trigger relayout. Good; keep that discipline for any FAB morph too.

Gate infinite background animation on lifecycle + reduced-motion — v1's rememberResumed()/rememberReducedMotion() gating of the aurora is exactly right for battery/GPU and accessibility. Keep it; just also respect it for shimmer.
Model UI as a sealed UiState (Loading/Empty/Content/Error), not a bare List plus swallowed runCatching — a designed empty/error state is what makes an app feel intentional rather than broken.

Widget: use system corner-radius tokens on API 31+ (@android:dimen/system_app_widget_background_radius) with a values fallback — v1 already does this in values-v31/dimens.xml. Add @android:color/system_accent1_* for Material You accent, same values-v31 pattern.

Widget rows: whole-row tap target + fill-in intents merged into one mutable PendingIntent template — v1 does this correctly (setPendingIntentTemplate + setOnClickFillInIntent), and the '+' opens an Activity directly to respect Android 12's ban on broadcast→activity trampolines. Preserve this.

code_patterns.what: M3 Expressive motion in the theme, then consumed everywhere (replaces the hand-typed springs in TaskRow/TodoScreen). Requires material3 1.5.0-alpha + opt-in.
TaskCheck with correct haptics + Expressive spring (v1 fires NO haptic today). Also flips the color spring to an effects spec.
Premium glass surface: distinct materials, specular top-edge highlight, inputScale, optional progressive fade. Upgrades GlassSurface.kt's single flat style.
Aurora with decorrelated blobs (v1 moves all three off one phase → mechanical). Give each blob its own frequency/phase so it drifts organically.
code_patterns.snippet:
val t by rememberInfiniteTransition("aurora").animateFloat(0f, 1f,
  infiniteRepeatable(tween(24_000, easing = LinearEasing), RepeatMode.Restart), label="t")
// per-blob: phase = fract(t * freq + offset); use sin/cos for smooth orbit
fun orbit(cx: Float, cy: Float, rx: Float, ry: Float, freq: Float, off: Float, size: Size) =
  Offset(size.width * (cx + rx * sin((t*freq+off)*2f*PI.toFloat())),
         size.height* (cy + ry * cos((t*freq+off)*2f*PI.toFloat())))
// blob1 freq 1.0, blob2 freq 0.6 off .33, blob3 freq 0.8 off .66 → no lockstep

code_patterns.what: Sealed UiState + shimmer skeleton (v1 has none — first launch is a bare aurora, failures are swallowed). Makes loading/empty/error intentional.
Widget Material You dynamic color (v1 has none). res/values-v31 override; keep hardcoded fallback in res/values for API 26-30.
code_patterns.snippet:
<!-- res/values-v31/colors.xml -->
<resources>
  <color name="widget_accent">@android:color/system_accent1_300</color>
  <color name="widget_accent_container">@android:color/system_accent2_700</color>
</resources>

  <color name="widget_accent">#35E0F0</color>
  <color name="widget_accent_container">#0A1E38</color>
</resources>
<!-- widget_add_bg.xml gradient uses @color/widget_accent; ic_check_on tint too -->

code_patterns.what: FAB/add-button morph done cheaply with graphicsLayer + interactionSource press scale (premium feel, no relayout). Apply to the bottom '+' IconButton.
code_patterns.snippet:
val interaction = remember { MutableInteractionSource() }
val pressed by interaction.collectIsPressedAsState()
val s by animateFloatAsState(if (pressed) 0.88f else 1f,
  MaterialTheme.motionScheme.fastSpatialSpec(), label="press")
IconButton(onClick = ..., interactionSource = interaction,
  modifier = Modifier.graphicsLayer { scaleX = s; scaleY = s })

our_current_mistakes: No haptics anywhere. TaskRow.kt TaskCheck toggles with clickable(onClick = onToggle) and never touches LocalHapticFeedback. For a 'premium' toggle this is the biggest single miss — Compose 1.8+ ships HapticFeedbackType.ToggleOn/ToggleOff (already in your BOM) built for exactly this.

Motion is hand-typed literals, not a system. TaskRow uses spring(Spring.DampingRatioMediumBouncy, Spring.StiffnessMedium) and tween(200); TodoScreen's animateItem uses more ad-hoc springs. There's no shared MotionScheme, so speeds/curves drift and don't match M3 Expressive. Centralize on MaterialTheme.motionScheme.

No loading/empty/error states in the APP. TodoScreen holds a raw var tasks: List<Todo> and does LaunchedEffect { runCatching { repo.list() }.getOrDefault(emptyList()) }. First launch = bare aurora with no skeleton; any network/auth failure silently yields an empty list indistinguishable from 'no tasks'. That reads as broken. Model a sealed UiState with Loading (shimmer), Empty (illustrative), Error (retry). Note the widget DOES have an empty view — the app is actually behind the widget here.

Glass is one flat style. GlassSurface.glass() applies the same HazeMaterials.thin + 24dp + single cyan tint to both the top bar and the input bar. No inputScale, no progressive/mask edge fade, no specular top-edge highlight, no material differentiation. It works but looks like a single frosted sheet rather than layered glass.

Aurora blobs move in lockstep. AuroraBackground drives all three radial gradients from ONE phase float, so they translate together on a single LinearEasing ramp — mechanical, not organic. Decorrelate with per-blob frequency/offset and sin/cos orbits.

Add flow refetches the whole list. In TodoScreen the '+' handler does repo.add(...) then tasks = repo.list() — a full round-trip that discards the optimistic-update pattern already used for toggles (tasks.map{...}). Append optimistically instead so the new row springs in via animateItem immediately.

Widget has no Material You and no completed-time affordance. widget_add_bg.xml and ic_check_on.xml hardcode #35E0F0 / #3B7EF5 instead of @android:color/system_accent1_* on API 31+, so the widget ignores the wallpaper palette that makes home-screen widgets feel native/premium. No due/relative time shown either.

reduced-motion is read once and never updates. rememberReducedMotion() reads ANIMATOR_DURATION_SCALE inside remember{} with no key, so toggling the accessibility setting at runtime won't take effect until process restart. Minor, but observe it via a settings ContentObserver if you want it live.

REQUEST_INSTALL_PACKAGES + allowBackup=true. Manifest requests install-packages (from the self-updater) and leaves allowBackup=true — unrelated to visual polish but worth flagging for a store-quality build; scope backup rules and gate the updater path.
release build has isMinifyEnabled = false. Not a UI issue, but ships an unshrunk/unobfuscated APK; enable R8 + a Compose baseline profile for smoother first-run frames on the animated screen.
pitfalls: Do NOT 'upgrade' Haze to 2.0 for this app. 1.7.2 is the current stable; 2.0.0-alpha03 is alpha and breaks your API surface (blur moves to a haze-blur module + dev.chrisbanes.haze.blur package, all blur props must move inside a blurEffect{} wrapper, HazeTint->HazeColorEffect, rememberHazeState(blurEnabled) removed). Migrating now buys nothing and risks alpha instability.

Blur only exists on API 31+. Haze uses RenderEffect; on minSdk 26-30 it silently degrades to a translucent scrim. Test on API 26-30 so the top/bottom bars still look deliberate (rely on tint + border, not blur) or they'll look flat and cheap on ~1/4 of installs.

Don't reach for per-row Haze to make the list 'more glassy'. That's the classic regression that tanks frame time (~+45% in Haze's benchmarks). The current translucent-card approach is correct; keep glass on the two bars.

MotionScheme is Expressive/alpha-gated. MaterialTheme.motionScheme + MotionScheme.expressive() require material3 1.5.0-alpha and @ExperimentalMaterial3ExpressiveApi. For zero alpha exposure, stay on 1.4.0 and hand-code spring specs that mirror Expressive values in one Motion.kt.

Shimmer/skeleton must respect reduced-motion and lifecycle, exactly like the aurora already does, or it burns battery and annoys motion-sensitive users. Freeze the gradient offset under reduced-motion.

Glance would NOT unlock blur for the widget. Neither RemoteViews nor Glance can sample/blur the wallpaper. Glance buys GlanceTheme + dynamic color + cleaner Kotlin but loses your working ListView collection ergonomics. Upgrading the existing RemoteViews (dynamic color, tighter gradient, previewImage) is higher ROI.
Progressive blur can regress older devices. If you add HazeProgressive, verify on a low-end/API 31 device; fall back to a mask brush (near-free) for the edge fade if it costs too much.

Widget PendingIntent mutability is load-bearing. The toggle template must stay FLAG_MUTABLE and the '+' must stay FLAG_IMMUTABLE opening an Activity directly. v1 gets this right — don't turn it into a broadcast->activity trampoline (Android 12 bans it).

Optimistic UI needs rollback. Once add() is optimistic (toggles already are), add a failure path that reverts local state on error, or a dropped write leaves the UI lying about server state — today runCatching swallows it silently.
